market_behavior.seasonality: Global consumption typically decreases by 1-2 million barrels daily in Q1 due to post-holiday industrial slowdowns and the end of winter heating. Demand accelerates sharply in Q2 and Q3, driven by the summer driving season and peak global aviation activity.
bullish_triggers: Coordinated OPEC+ production cuts designed to drain on-water inventories
Geopolitical conflicts threatening major maritime transit chokepoints like the Strait of Hormuz
bearish_triggers: Higher interest rate environments that suppress industrial manufacturing capacity
Continuous accumulation of floating storage and on-water sanctioned oil
Aggressive non-OPEC+ production growth generating structural inventory overhangs

historical_crisis_patterns.market_reaction: The closure trapped millions of barrels in the Persian Gulf, forcing nations to shut in production due to an inability to safely transit tankers. Brent crude surged from $61 to $118 per barrel, widening the spread with WTI to an unprecedented $25.

Prolonged freezing paralyzed the Texas energy grid and forced the shut-in of over 1 million barrels per day of crude output. This tightened physical markets globally, establishing a persistent bullish premium as international refineries aggressively drew down existing inventories.

feedstocks: Associated gas from crude oil extraction

market_behavior.seasonality: Demand experiences intense, inelastic peaks during the winter heating season (Nov-Mar), with a secondary peak in mid-summer for electric cooling. The temperate shoulder seasons see waning demand, allowing for inventory storage injections.
bullish_triggers: Late-season Arctic cold snaps that rapidly draw down depleted storage
Unexpected mechanical outages at major LNG liquefaction facilities
Severe winter weather events causing wellhead freeze-offs
bearish_triggers: Warmer-than-normal winter temperatures heavily suppressing space-heating demand
Excessive associated gas production from active crude oil drilling operations
Stranded molecules due to overwhelmed local pipeline takeaway capacity

historical_crisis_patterns.market_reaction: Deep Arctic cold drove record heating demand while freezing un-winterized wellheads, plunging Texas production by 15%. The resulting systemic grid collapse spiked Henry Hub spot prices to an astronomical $23.86/MMBtu, forcing heavy industries into immediate force majeure.

Following the war in Ukraine, Russian pipeline gas flows to Europe were curtailed by over half. Panic-buying of global LNG surged TTF prices above €300/MWh, resulting in a staggering 19% structural demand destruction across European energy-intensive industries.

market_behavior.seasonality: Demand tightens during the summer months as refiners dynamically divert light naphtha streams into the gasoline blending pool to meet peak driving season requirements, elevating petrochemical procurement costs.
bullish_triggers: Robust consumer demand for end-use plastics and synthetic textiles
Supply disruptions in parallel petrochemical feedstocks like LPG
Elevated crude oil input costs and planned refinery turnarounds
bearish_triggers: Macroeconomic downturns depressing industrial and consumer demand for plastics
Overproduction by major Middle Eastern refining complexes resulting in export supply gluts
Reduced gasoline blending requirements during the winter months

historical_crisis_patterns.market_reaction: The Strait of Hormuz closure and Qatari LPG halts forced petrochemical plants to aggressively substitute with naphtha. Prices skyrocketed 88% to $918/tonne, causing rare, devastating negative-margin inversions and force majeures across Asian plastic producers.

Sanctions removed vast volumes of Russian naphtha from European and Asian markets, forcing a desperate pivot to alternative supplies. Bidding wars for Middle Eastern and US cargoes drove Asian refining margins to multi-year highs of $173 per tonne.

market_behavior.seasonality: Prices typically soften during regional harvest periods (autumn in the US, spring in Brazil) due to peak feedstock availability. Demand tracks standard gasoline seasonality, spiking during the summer driving season.
bullish_triggers: Agricultural distress such as prolonged droughts, floods, or early frosts
Governmental policy shifts increasing mandatory renewable fuel blending volumes
Surges in global crude oil and wholesale gasoline prices
bearish_triggers: Bumper agricultural harvests flooding the market with cheap feedstock
Steep declines in global sugar prices incentivizing mills to maximize ethanol distillation
Aggressive dumping of discounted export cargoes into international hubs

historical_crisis_patterns.market_reaction: Severe drought and frosts decimated Brazil's sugarcane yields, reducing crushed volume by over 5%. The supply shock sent hydrous ethanol prices soaring 50%, exacerbated by mills artificially sustaining the pricing floor by heavily hedging towards high-priced sugar instead.

Excellent agricultural weather and crashing international sugar prices led to a massive 36.9 billion liter overproduction cycle in Brazil. Wholesale prices plummeted, causing a severe margin crush for producers globally as supply vastly outpaced domestic and export demand.

market_behavior.seasonality: Demand and pricing peak massively during the summer driving season (Q2-Q3) due to increased consumer travel and the higher production cost of legally mandated, less-volatile summer-blend formulations.
bullish_triggers: Cyber-physical attacks on critical midstream pipeline distribution infrastructure
Hurricane landfalls in major refining hubs forcing the preventative shutdown of distillation units
Unplanned refinery maintenance, fires, or force majeures reducing regional output
bearish_triggers: Severe macroeconomic recessions that aggressively curtail consumer spending and mobility
High domestic inventory builds reported during the winter months
Unexpected releases from strategic national petroleum reserves

historical_crisis_patterns.market_reaction: A highly targeted ransomware attack forced operators to shut down physical systems on the primary US East Coast fuel artery for six days. The localized supply chain collapse triggered massive panic buying, drained retail stations, and spiked national average prices to a 7-year high of $3.04 per gallon.
